coredata.dc:title: Effect of symmetry and substituents of cobalt based phthalocyanines in aptasensor design for the electrochemical impedimetric detection of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2
coredata.dc:creator: Centane, Sixolile
Mgidlana, Sithi
Openda, Yolande
Ndebele, Nobuhle
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Surface functionalization in biosensor design remains the major contributor to biosensor efficiency and selectivity. In electrochemical biosensor design, the surface is usually functionalized to achieve immobilization of the biorecognition element as well as electron transfer mediation for signal amplification. An asymmetric cobalt tris-(acetylphenoxy)-mono-phenoxyacrylic acid phthalocyanine (CoMPhAaPc) and a symmetric cobalt tetra-acetylphenoxy phthalocyanine were used for immobilization of HB5 aptamer and signal amplification towards the electrochemical detection of the human epidermal growth factor 2 (HER2). The efficiency of the two phthalocyanines is compared in relation to the substituents and symmetry. The two phthalocyanines were further coupled with sulphur nitrogen graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) via π-π interactions, for enhanced activity and performance towards HER2 detection. The efficiency of the designed sensors was tested using the detection of HER2 in human serum. The best performance was observed for the SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc/HB5 (amide) with limit of detection value of 0.0027 ng/mL and sensitivity of 72,253 Ωng−1mL. Considering these results, the aptasensors demonstrate great potential for improved monitoring of human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 levels for the management of breast cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt phthalocyanine
Human epidermal growth factor 2
Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy
Aptamer
Electrocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the cobalt tris – (acetylphenoxy)-mono-phenoxyacrylic acid phthalocyanine (CoMPhAaPc).
HER2 detection in human serum samples
Characterization of phthalocyanines and their composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Republic of South Africa

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) and National Research Foundation through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University , and DSI/Mintek Nanotechnology Innovation Center-Sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical Structure of the cobalt tetra-acetylphenoxy (CoTAcPhPc).
UV–Vis spectra of (a) CoMPhAaPc in THF, (b) SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc in DMSO, (c) SNGQDs in water, (d) CoTAcPhPc in DMSO and (e) SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc in DMSO.
Raman spectra of SNGQDs (a), SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc (b) and SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc (c).

(A) CV at the scan rate of 100 mV/s and (B) EIS Nyquist plots for the probes: GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2), GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4), GCE/SNGQDs(π) CoMPhAaPc(3) and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5). All experiments were conducted in Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1 mM in 0.1 mol·L−1 KCl in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4). Inserts = (A) CV and (B) EIS (bottom insert) for bare electrode. The circuit employed for EIS is shown as top insert in B.

Nyquist plots and regression plots of log HER2 vs. Rct for the probes (A, B) GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine), (C, D) GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(imine), (E, F) GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4)/HB5 and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5)/HB5 (G, H). All in Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1 mM in 0.1 mol·L−1 KCl in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4).

Shelf-life estimation of the various sensors over 96 h. The stability of the probes upon interaction with HER2 ([HER2] = 5 ng/mL, n = 3). Experiments were run in triplicate: error bars for each day represent the SDEV calculated for the n = 3 per day for each probe. Values above bars are % RSD values obtained over 4 days for each labelled probe. All in Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1 mM in 0.1 mol·L−1 KCl in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4).
Steps towards the synthesis of the CoMPhAaPc.

Steps towards the formation of the conjugates, showing SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc as an example. Furthermore, the immobilization technique on the GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc (5) to form aptasensor GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5)/HB5 is shown. Dil = dilute.
Steps towards the fabrication of the GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(amide) and GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine) aptasensors for the detection of HER2.
ΔEp and Rct values in Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (1 mM in 0.1 mol·L−1 KCl in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4).
Limit of detection (LOD) and sensitivities for detection of HER2 on biosensors developed in this work. Linear range 1–10 ng/mL in all cases.
Comparison of LOD and linear range for HER2 biosensors developed in this work with those reported in literature.

row.entry: Label Free Immunosensor (antibody fragments)-screen printed electrode

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CoTAPc = Cobalt tetra acetic acid phthalocyanine, CoTCPPc = Cobalt tetracarboxy phenoxy phthalocyanine, NPs = nanoparticles, MWCNTs = multiwalled carbon nanotubes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Table of obtained percentage recoveries of HER2 detection in human serum.

head.ce:title: Effect of symmetry and substituents of cobalt based phthalocyanines in aptasensor design for the electrochemical impedimetric detection of the human epidermal growth factor receptor 2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140, South Africa
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, Makhanda 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface functionalization in biosensor design remains the major contributor to biosensor efficiency and selectivity. In electrochemical biosensor design, the surface is usually functionalized to achieve immobilization of the biorecognition element as well as electron transfer mediation for signal amplification. An asymmetric cobalt tris-(acetylphenoxy)-mono-phenoxyacrylic acid phthalocyanine (CoMPhAaPc) and a symmetric cobalt tetra-acetylphenoxy phthalocyanine were used for immobilization of HB5 aptamer and signal amplification towards the electrochemical detection of the human epidermal growth factor 2 (HER2). The efficiency of the two phthalocyanines is compared in relation to the substituents and symmetry. The two phthalocyanines were further coupled with sulphur nitrogen graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) via π-π interactions, for enhanced activity and performance towards HER2 detection. The efficiency of the designed sensors was tested using the detection of HER2 in human serum. The best performance was observed for the SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc/HB5 (amide) with limit of detection value of 0.0027 ng/mL and sensitivity of 72,253 Ωng−1mL. Considering these results, the aptasensors demonstrate great potential for improved monitoring of human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 levels for the management of breast cancer.

ce:section.ce:para: The two phthalocyanines were explored as electrode modifiers to first, improve the sensing abilities of the glassy carbon electrode. Secondly, as immobilization platforms for an HB5 aptamer as the biorecognition element. The two phthalocyanines were also combined with sulphur nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) via π-π interactions. GQDs enhance the activity of other electrocatalysts such as MPcs [4,5]. It has also been reported that doping of pristine graphene quantum dots with heteroatoms such as nitrogen [16] and sulfur [17] can further enhance electron transfer, hence SNGQDs are employed in this work.

The analyte of interest in this work is the established breast cancer biomarker, HER2. To ensure, high sensitivity and specificity of the designed sensors; an HER2 specific DNA aptamer (HB5) is used as a biorecognition element. The electrode of choice for modification and sensing is the glassy carbon electrode (GCE).

The following materials were purchased Sigma Aldrich: human serum (H6914), bovine serum albumin (BSA), N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), N, N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaCl, KCl, K4[Fe(CN)6], K3[Fe(CN)6], dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), 1-pentanol, 1, 8-diazabicyclo (5.4.0) undec-7-ene (DBU), cobalt acetate, and the protein (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2, HER2). Nitric acid, sulphuric acid, and 25 % ammonia were purchased from B and M Scientific. The HB5 DNA aptamer (5′-/5 AmMC6/AAC CGC CCA AAT CCC TAA GAG TCT GCA CTT GTC ATT TTG TAT ATG TAT TTG GTT TTT GGC TCT CAC AGA CAC ACT ACA CAC GCA CA-3′) was purchased from Integrated DNA technologies (IDT), South Africa. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was prepared using weighed amounts of Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, KCl and NaCl in ultra-pure water from ELGA, Veolia PURELAB, flex system (Marlow, UK). The cobalt tetra-acetylphenoxy (CoTAcPhPc) [10] and the sulphur – nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (SNGQDs) [18] were synthesized as reported in literature. The syntheses of (E) -3-(3-(3,4-dicyanophenoxy) phenyl) acrylic acid [19] and 4-(3-acetylphenoxy) phthalonitrile [20] were as reported in literature.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) studies were performed using an Autolab Potentiostat PGSTAT30 equipped with Nova software version 2.1. The EIS experiments were performed between 0.1 and 100000 Hz, using 0.01 VRMS sinusoidal modulation. A non-linear least squares (NLLS) method based on the EQUIVCRT programme was used for automatic fitting of the obtained EIS data. A Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064 nm Nd: YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector) was used to collect Raman data. Elemental analysis was done using a Vario-Elementar Microcube® ELIII, while mass spectral data were collected on a Bruker® AutoFLEXIII smart-beam TOF/TOF mass spectrometer using α-cyano-4-hydrocinnamic acid as the matrix in the positive ion mode. Ground state electronic absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the cobalt tris – (acetylphenoxy)-mono-phenoxyacrylic acid phthalocyanine (CoMPhAaPc).
ce:section.ce:para:
CoMPhAaPc was synthesized as depicted in Scheme 1. In a reaction flask containing cobalt acetate (44 mg, 0.25 mmol), a stoichiometric mixture (1:3) of (E) -3-(3-(3,4-dicyanophenoxy) phenyl) acrylic acid (0.25 g, 0.861 mmol) and 4-(3-acetylphenoxy) phthalonitrile (0.67 g, 2.583 mmol) was added. The contents were then dissolved in 1-pentanol (5 mL), accompanied by dropwise addition of DBU (0.2 mL). The reaction was subject to heating at 160 °C for 9 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The resulting product (CoMPhAaPc) was precipitated out using methanol (100 %). The product was further purified using chromatographic methods on a Si60 column with methanol/THF (1: 5 ratio). Yield: 0.48 g (48.9 %). UV/Vis (DMSO): λmax, nm, (log ε), 666 (5.32), 600 (4.96) and 376 (5.55). IR (Platinum ATR): νmax , cm−1, 3005 (OH), 1750 (C
O) and 2925 (C
H). MALDI–TOF MS m/z: Calcd.: 1135.99 Found: ([M + 3H]) 1139.20. Elemental Anal. Calc. for C65H40CoN8O9 C:68.72, N:9.86 Found C: 67.21 N: 9.52.
2.3.2 Non-covalent π – π stacking of the SNGQDs to the CoMPhAaPc and CoTAcPhPc, Scheme 2 .

The SNGQDs and the cobalt phthalocyanines are highly π - conjugated nanomaterials, and as a result can undergo π-π interactions. The π-π stacking of the SNGQDs with the Pc was done as follows: SNGQDs (5 mL, 1 mg/mL in H2O) were added to a CoMPhAaPc (5 mL, 4.424 × 10−3 mmol in DMF) or CoTAcPhPc (5 mL, 4.55 × 10−3 mmol in DMF) solutions. The resulting mixtures were subjected to stirring to ensure complete mixing of the SNQGDs and the respective CoMPhAaPc and CoTAcPhPc solutions. Following that, the two mixtures were subject to sonication for 4 h at room temperature to form SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc and SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc.

All the solutions were freshly prepared in Milli-Q water and stored at the temperature of 4 °C until use. Solutions were deaerated by bubbling argon gas prior to the electrochemistry experiments and the electrochemical cell was kept under argon atmosphere throughout the experiments. Glassy carbon electrodes were first polished with Al2O3 powder (Aldrich, 0.1, 0.05 mm) using Metrohm polishing kit, and rinsed with deionized water, followed by sonication in ethanol and deionized water. Finally, the electrodes were allowed to dry at room temperature.

The GCEs were modified by the drop and dry method where the modifier was dropped on the electrode followed by drying as follows: the freshly cleaned GCE was modified using (10 µL, 1 mg/mL SNQGDs in water), CoMPhAaPc (10 µL, 8.85 × 10−4 mmol, DMF), CoTAcPhPc (10 µL, 9.09 × 10−4 mmol in, DMF), SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc (10 µL, 1 mg/L in DMF) and SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc (10 µL, 1 mg/mL, DMF). The resulting electrodes are presented as: GCE/SNGQDs(1), GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2), GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3), GCE/ CoTAcPhPc(4), and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5). The different modified electrodes are represented using numbers (in brackets) for ease of reference.

The recognition element of choice in this work is the HB5 aptamer, with an amino functionalized terminal. All nanomaterials were synthesized with functional groups to afford covalent linkage with the HB5 aptamer. All modified electrodes: GCE/SNGQDs(1), GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2), GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3), GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4) and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5) were subject to interaction with the HB5 aptamer as governed by the functional groups within each nanomaterial. The SNGQDs, CoMPhAaPc and SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc nanomaterials have carboxylic terminals, in which the amide linkage with HB5 was achieved using DCC/NHS coupling, Scheme 3 (showing GCE/ CoMPhAaPc as an example). To the modified electrodes GCE/SNGQDs(1), GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2) and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3) modified electrodes: DCC (6.0 mg, 0.029 mmol) and NHS (4.0 mg, 0.035 mmol) were added, the electrodes were left at room temperature for 1 h. HB5 aptamer (10 µL, 1.0 µM in PBS) was then dropped onto the DCC/NHS activated surfaces giving surfaces: GCE/SNGQDs(1)/HB5, GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(amide) and GCE/SNGQDs(π) CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide). Considering that the CoMPhAaPc has one carboxylic and three ketone functional groups; the immobilization of the HB5 aptamer on the GCE/CoMPhAaPc via its ketone ligands was also explored, Scheme 3. CoTAcPhPc has ketone functional groups only in which case the immobilization of the HB5 aptamer on the GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4) and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5) was achieved following the acid catalysed imine formation reaction. Imine bonds typically form upon reaction of a ketone (e.g. CoTAcPhPc) and a primary amine (e.g. HB5 aptamer) in the presence of a mild acid, Scheme 3. For electrodes (2,3,4,5) acid catalysed imine formation reaction was achieved as follows: 10 µL of HB5 aptamer was dropped on the modified electrodes, followed by addition of 10 µL of (dilute H2SO4, pH = 4.5), and then the electrodes were left to dry in the oven. The resulting aptasensors are represented as: GCE/ CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine), GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(imine), GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4)/HB5 and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5)/HB5. The electrode numbers are in brackets. The CoMPhAaPc based aptasensors are denoted as GCE/ CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5 (amide) and GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5 (imine) to differentiate on the mechanism of HB5 immobilization. Similar labelling was used for the conjugates and are denoted as GCE/ SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide) and GCE/SNGQDs (π) CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(imine).

A total of seven aptasensors: GCE/SNGQDs(1)/HB5, GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(amide), GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine), GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide), GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(imine), GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4)/HB5 and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5)/HB5 were designed and investigated towards the electrochemical detection of HER2. All seven electrodes were then treated with 1% BSA (bovine serum albumin) to prevent non-specific binding, and again rinsed with PBS.

The electrochemical response of the electrodes in the presence of varying concentrations of the HER2 antigen was measured using the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The EIS measurement was conducted under the stable open circuit potential with the frequency from 0.1 to 100000 Hz, using 0.01 VRMS sinusoidal modulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HER2 detection in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary experiments for the determination of HER2 protein in human serum samples were also performed. The designed aptasensors were tested in 1/500 diluted (in 0.1 M PBS buffer, pH 7.4) serum samples spiked by standard addition of HER2 protein (1 ng/mL, 3 ng/mL, 5 ng/mL and 7 ng/mL). The response of the aptasensors was then determined by EIS measurements, under the same conditions used for HER2 calibration curve.

The repeatability of the designed probes was investigated by running successive measurements at each HER2 concentrations in triplicate and the standard deviation was calculated. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for all electrodes was determined at various concentrations, Table S1. The stability of the designed sensors was assessed in terms of storage and/or shelf life time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of phthalocyanines and their composites
ce:section.ce:para: The electronic absorption spectra of the two phthalocyanines CoMPhAaPc and CoTAcPhPc (in the absence of SNGQDs) showed Q- bands at 664 nm and 677 nm, and Soret bands at 327 nm and 331 nm, respectively, Fig. 2 a, d. The Q bands are broad, typical of CoPc derivatives [9]. The Q band is red shifted for CoTAcPhPc due to the presence of four acetyl phenoxy groups which are electron donating, instead of only three of these groups in CoMPhAaPc. The SNGQDs had an absorption peak at around 349 nm, which is similar to previously reported S and N co-doped graphene based quantum dots [21], Fig. 2c. There were no significant spectral shifts in the Q-bands of the composites: SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc and SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc (Fig. 2b, e) compared to the respective CoMPhAaPc and CoTAcPhPc. An increased absorbance and slight broadening was observed for the Soret Bands of both Pc upon conjugation with the SNGQDs. This can be attributed to the overlap in the absorbance of the SNGQDs and Soret bands. Furthermore, the Q-bands of both Pcs retained their absorption peak position and intensity even following conjugation, suggesting that the π-π stacking conjugation does not compromise the structural integrity of the Pcs.

The structural integrity of the SNGQDs before and after conjugation was assessed using Raman Spectroscopy, Fig. 3 . GQDs shows two common Raman modes: the D and G bands [22]. The G-band is the primary mode in graphene and represents the planar configuration sp2 bonded carbon, while the d-band is the defect or disorder band originating in structural defects, edge effects and dangling sp2 carbon bonds that break the symmetry [23,24]. The D and G bands were positioned respectively at 1312 cm−1; 1595 cm−1 for SNGQDs, 1262 cm−1; 1432 cm−1 for SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc and 1308 cm−1; 1507 cm−1 for SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc. A major shift was observed for the SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc sample, compared to that of the SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc. Raman peaks for graphene quantum dots occurring at ∼1260 cm−1 and 1430 cm−1 have been reported and associated with edge functional groups, COOH or CO groups [25]. Thus, the presence of the extra COOH group in CoMPhAaPc in addition to the COOH groups in SNGQDs, could have resulted in the shift for SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc. The ID/IG ratio was calculated for SNGQDs, SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc and SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc to be 1.39, 0.23 and 0.56, respectively. Thus, the additional π electrons from the Pc decreased the ID/IG ratio.

EIS is well known as an effective tool for studying the properties of surface modified electrodes [14]. EIS measurements were carried out for all modified electrodes. The Nyquist plots obtained are shown and labelled accordingly in Fig. 4B, with the obtained Rct values obtained listed in Table 1. The data obtained were fitted using a modified equivalent circuit. The key parameter used to differentiate and characterize the modified electrodes was the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which increases with decrease in conductivity and vice versa. The obtained data for the EIS data were in agreement with that obtained in cyclic voltammetry for the modified electrodes: the modified surface with the lowest ΔEp had the lowest Rct values, Table 1.

The following HB5 containing electrodes: GCE/SNGQDs(1)/HB5, GCE/CoMAcPhPc(2)/HB5(amide), GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide), GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine), GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(imine), GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4)/HB5 and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5)/HB5, were all tested toward the electrochemical detection of HER2 at varying concentrations of 1 ng to 10 ng. The performance of the probes was investigated and compared using EIS. Fig. 5 (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information) show the Nyquist plots for HER2 detection in PBS. The figures show that the Rct values increased with the HER2 concentration in the tested linear range. Table S1 provides the data. Regression plots were then created using the data of each electrode, the equations of the regression plots are as follows:
(i)
GCE/SNGQDs(1)/HB5:
y = 9650.4 x - 5117 ,
R
2
= 0.9233
(ii)
GCE/ CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(amide):
y = 12911 x + 29075 ,
R
2
= 0.9618
(iii)
GCE/ CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine):
y = 30262 x + 15843 ,
R
2
= 0.9886
(iv)
GCE/ CoMPhAaPc(π)SNGQDs(3)/HB5(amide):
y = 72253 x + 7389.2 ,
R
2
= 0.9711
(v)
GCE/ CoMPhAaPc(π)SNGQDs(3)/HB5(imine):
y = 34245 x + 24472 ,
R
2
= 0.9623
(vi)
GCE/ CoTAcPhPc 4)/HB5:
y = 16414 x + 39607 ,
R
2
= 0.9703
(vii)
GCE/ CoTAcPhPc(π)SNGQDs(5)/HB5:
y = 20553 x + 12427 ,
R
2
= 0.9652

The slope = m of the plots represents the sensitivity for the respective aptasensors, Table 2 . The aptasensor GCE/CoMPhAaPc2)/HB5(amide) had a sensitivity value of 12,911 Ωng−1mL, which increased to 72,253 Ωng−1mL when conjugated to SNGQDs forming the sensor GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide). In these two aptasensors the HB5 aptamer was immobilized via an amide bond formation aided by DCC/NHS coupling. In the case of GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(amide) the HB5 aptamer was immobilized via the single phenoxyacrylic acid ligand, leaving the three acetylphenoxy groups unoccupied, Scheme 3. The improvement in sensitivity for the GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide) aptasensor could be attributed to increased electron delocalization between the SNGQDs and CoMPhAaPc in the conjugate. Furthermore, the SNQGDs have carboxylic groups on their periphery which are also activated when the GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3) is treated with DCC/NHS for HB5 immobilization. This could result in more HB5 aptamers on the surface of GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide) for sensing HER2 hence the higher sensitivity observed. GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine) had a sensitivity of 30,262 Ωng−1mL, over twice as much as that of GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(amide). For the aptasensor GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine), HB5 was immobilized on the three acetylphenoxy ligands via imine bond formation, while for GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(amide) only one substituent is involved for immobilization of HB5. The results demonstrate the significance of having more ligands for HB5 linking as well as the efficiency of the phenoxyacrylic acid ligand in electron distribution to the Pc and its interaction with the electrolyte for signal generation. The aptasensor GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(imine), which is the SNGQDs and CoMPhAaPc conjugate where the HB5 aptamer is linked via imine bond formation had a sensitivity of 34,245 Ωng−1mL. In which case, only the Pc partakes as an immobilization platform for HB5, while the SNGQDs sole purpose is electron delocalization and electron transfer. The improvement in sensitivity obtained, further demonstrates the significance of combining various nanomaterials for signal amplification in sensor design. The GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4)/HB5 aptasensor had a sensitivity of 16,414 Ωng−1mL, while the conjugated based aptasensor GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5)/HB5 had a sensitivity of 20,553 Ωng−1mL.

The limit of detection (LOD) of the designed aptasensors was then calculated using the 3σ/s equation, where σ is the standard deviation of Rct values obtained for PBS (without HER2) for all probes, and s is the slope of the regression plots. The best LOD was obtained for the GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide) at 2.72 × 10−3 ng/mL, which also had the highest sensitivity, Table 2. The asymmetrical GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine) gave a lower LOD than the symmetrical GCE/CoTAcPhPc (4)/HB5, corresponding to the high sensitivity for the former and showing the importance of reduced symmetry. The same applies in the presence of SNGQDs. The phthalocyanine based sensors GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(amide) and GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4)/HB5 probes had better LOD values 0.33 ng/mL and 0.24 ng/mL respectively, compared to our previously reported GCE/CoTCPhPc (Co(II) tetracarboxy phenoxy phthalocyanine)/HB5 (0.647 ng/mL) towards HER2 detection [28], Table 3 . Similarly, the GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine) probe showed lower detection value (0.0039 ng/mL) than a previously reported Co tetra acetic acid Pc (CoTAPc)/HB5 (0.051 ng/mL) towards HER2 detection [11], Table 1. The GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide), had a lower LOD value (at 0.0027 ng/mL) than a previously reported sensor based on a phthalocyanine combined with nanoparticles: GCE/CoTAPc/CeO2NPs/HB5 [11] (at 0.008 ng/mL), Table 3. The performance of the designed aptasensors in this work, proved comparable and sometimes superior to what has been reported in literature, for the electrochemical detection of HER2 [11,28–32].

The repeatability of the designed aptasensors is demonstrated throughout, the experiments were run (n = 3) at all concentrations for all ten electrodes, Table S1. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for all electrodes at various concentrations was determined. Acceptable values for biosensor designed were obtained for all probes.

The stability of the listed probes above, was determined using the shelf-life estimation method. [HER2] = 5 ng/mL in 10 Mm PBS (pH 7.4) was employed in all cases. The probes were stored at 4 °C. After every 24-h period, the stability of the sensor probes was investigated using EIS and measuring the Rct values in 1 mM Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in 0.1 mol·L−1 KCl in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4). The results obtained for the following probes: GCE/SNGQDs(1)/HB5, GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine), GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(imine), GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4)/HB5 and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5)/HB5 are shown in Fig. 6 . The degradation on each surface was assessed using % relative standard deviation (RSD) values obtained from every 24 h until the 96th h. For each aptasensor, each measurement was run in triplicate in each day until day 4. The aptasensor GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine) showed better stability compared to the GCE/CoTAcPhPc(4)/HB5 aptasensor. The conjugate based probes GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(imine) and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5)/HB5 showed better stability when compared to the respective Pc are used without SNGQDs. Between the two conjugate based probes: the GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(imine) showed better stability compared to the GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5)/HB5 probe. The probes GCE/(2–5)/HB5 showed great storage stability over a 96 h period, with acceptable %RSD for biosensors.

The potential applicability of the designed sensors in the analysis of real samples was investigated by HER2 determination in human serum. Where, different concentration of HER2 were added to healthy human serum samples. The results obtained from the detection of HER2 in human serum samples for selected probes are assembled in Table 4 (the rest of the data shown in supplementary information, Table S2). The accuracy and precision of the various aptasensors was evaluated by % recovery and the relative standard deviation (RSD) calculation respectively. The recovery amounts were higher when the respective phthalocyanines were combine with the SNGQDs. The best recovery amounts were obtained for the GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide) in the range of 83 %−111 %. The best percentage recovery for phthalocyanines alone based probes was observed for the GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(amide) in the range 73 %–101 %. Recovery amount percentage obtained below 80 % could be due to the matrix effect [33]. The relative standard deviations were less than 8 %, revealing the utilization potentiality of the various aptasensor towards the detection of HER2 in the diagnosis of breast cancer.

In this paper we report on the effect of symmetry and substituents on two cobalt based phthalocyanines towards the electrochemical detection of HER2. The two phthalocyanines, asymmetric (CoMPhAaPc) and symmetric (CoTAcPhPc) were used for immobilization of HB5 aptamer and signal amplification. Furthermore, the two phthalocyanines were coupled with SNGQDs for enhanced activity and performance towards HER2 detection. The ligands present on the molecule even after immobilization do not only contribute to symmetry, but electron transfer properties which too is a major contributor to signal generation. The best performance of the aptasensors containing Pc only was observed for the GCE/CoMPhAaPc(2)/HB5(imine) in terms of sensitivity and LOD value. The results obtained for all conjugate based sensors demonstrated the positive effects of combining two or more nanomaterials for improved detection signals of various sensors. GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(amide), had higher sensitivity and lower LOD value compared to GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoMPhAaPc(3)/HB5(imine) and GCE/SNGQDs(π)CoTAcPhPc(5)/HB5 counter probes. The designed sensors showed excellent results in the detection of HER2 in buffer and human serum sample. The sensors designed in this work showed good linearity, sensitivity, repeatability with detection limits less than the 2–15 ng/mL HER2 cut off. The sensors showed sensitivity towards the HER2 protein and have considerable potential as an effective alternative method for the early detection of HER2.

Sixolile Centane: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Sithi Mgidlana: Methodology. Yolande Openda: Data curation. Nobuhle Ndebele: Methodology. Tebello Nyokong: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Innovation (DSI) and National Research Foundation through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), Rhodes University, and DSI/Mintek Nanotechnology Innovation Center-Sensors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2023.117524.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
